message:
beads: file 17l4c (DecisionReceipt missing integrity protection, P2)

File flywheel_connectors-17l4c open/P2/bug describing a signature gap
on DecisionReceipt in crates/fcp-audit/src/lib.rs:743. The finding,
full reasoning, and fix plan are captured verbatim in the bead body —
too long to repeat here without losing fidelity, and the tracker is
the source of truth.

Short version: DecisionReceipt has no issuer_kid / signature fields,
so the content-addressed "why allowed/denied" record that drives
`fcp explain` is trusted-but-unauthenticated. A middleware process
that mutates decision, reason_code, or explanation in flight is
indistinguishable from the legitimate issuer. The audit_entry_id
link to a signed AuditEntry is advisory only (Optional, a plain
String, not cryptographically bound to the receipt's fields).

Fix plan inside the bead mirrors the AuditEntry signing pattern
(issuer_kid: Option<KeyId>, signature: Option<Ed25519Signature>, +
sign/verify methods following the domain-separated-transcript shape
already used at crates/fcp-audit/src/lib.rs:1432).

Filed from a multi-pass static-analysis pass over fcp-audit
(signature/verify gaps sub-pass).

Pure metadata. No source files.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

+{"id":"flywheel_connectors-17l4c","title":"fcp-audit: DecisionReceipt has no integrity protection — no signature, no issuer_kid field","description":"## Finding

`crates/fcp-audit/src/lib.rs:743` defines `DecisionReceipt` — the content-addressed "why allowed/denied" record that powers `fcp explain`. The struct has 13 fields: id, request_id, decision, reason_code, evidence, audit_entry_id, explanation, decided_at, zone_id, correlation_id, trace_context, connector_id, operation_id. None of them are a signature, MAC, or issuer kid.

Compare to `AuditEntry` in the same file at line 177 which DOES carry integrity protection:

```
pub issuer_kid: Option<KeyId>,   // line 216
pub signature: Option<Ed25519Signature>,  // line 225
```

`DecisionReceipt` has neither.

## Why this is P2

1. **Receipts are trusted-but-unauthenticated.** A downstream reader (CLI, dashboard, SIEM) that ingests a `DecisionReceipt` JSON has no cryptographic way to verify the host's policy engine actually issued it. A middle-ware process that mutates `decision`, `reason_code`, or `explanation` in flight is indistinguishable from the legitimate issuer.

2. **The `audit_entry_id` link is advisory only.** The receipt has `audit_entry_id: Option<String>` linking back to the signed audit chain entry, which MIGHT provide indirect authentication — except the field is:
    - Optional (a receipt with `audit_entry_id: None` has no integrity link at all)
    - A plain String (attacker can forge any id they want)
    - Not covered by any hash-of-receipt-fields check that would bind the receipt content to the audit entry's content-addressed id

   So even when `audit_entry_id` is Some, an attacker can mutate the receipt's `reason_code` / `explanation` / `evidence` fields without invalidating the link. The audit entry remains signed; the receipt alongside it can carry a lie about what that entry said.

3. **This is the operator-facing surface.** Unlike AuditEntry (which lives inside the host's own chain), DecisionReceipts are what operators read and agents consume via `fcp explain`. The expected trust model is "a receipt that validates proves the policy engine authorised this" — current implementation breaks that contract silently.

## Fix plan

Add optional `issuer_kid` + `signature` fields paralleling `AuditEntry`:

```rust
#[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
pub issuer_kid: Option<KeyId>,
#[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
pub signature: Option<Ed25519Signature>,
```

Plus `DecisionReceipt::sign(&Ed25519SigningKey)` and `::verify(&Ed25519VerifyingKey)` methods that follow the `AuditEntry` signing pattern exactly (domain-separated transcript over the canonical CBOR of the remaining fields, kid included in the transcript).

## Related

- fcp-host's policy engine is where `DecisionReceipt` values originate; that crate would need the signing key and call `.sign()` before emit.
- `fcp-audit::verify_chain_with_signers` (lib.rs:1432) is the model for the verify-time signature check; a `DecisionReceipt` verifier would follow the same shape.

## Filed from

Multi-pass static analysis of `fcp-audit` (Pass 3: signature/verify gaps). Findings are grep-evident and not behind any feature flag.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:37:15.015359Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:37:15.015359Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh.4","title":"[Registry] Verify GitHub Actions release workflow produces cross-platform binaries","description":"The .github/workflows/release.yml (11KB) exists but has never been demonstrated to produce artifacts. Verify: 1) Trigger the release workflow (manually or via tag), 2) Verify it builds for Linux x86_64 + aarch64, macOS x86_64 + aarch64, 3) Verify binaries are signed, 4) Verify checksums are generated, 5) Verify a GitHub Release is created with all artifacts attached. If the workflow fails, fix it. This is the distribution pipeline that makes FCP installable by users outside this repository.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"SunnyMoose","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:53:53.483847Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T06:13:08.765823Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T06:13:08.765459Z","close_reason":"Added missing Windows ARM64 release matrix entries to release workflow for CLI/host and connector artifacts","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","registry"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh.4","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:53:53.483847Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2747,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh.4","author":"jemanuel","text":"SunnyMoose verification of .github/workflows/release.yml (2026-04-18):

WORKFLOW STRUCTURE — CORRECT:
1. Triggers: push tags v*, workflow_dispatch with version input ✓
2. Concurrency: group=release, cancel-in-progress=false ✓
3. Build matrix: 5 platforms (Linux x64+ARM64, macOS x64+ARM64, Windows x64) ✓
4. Packages: tar.xz (Unix), zip (Windows) ✓
5. Checksums: SHA256 per-artifact + combined SHA256SUMS.txt ✓
6. Signing: Sigstore cosign keyless (OIDC) ✓
7. Release: softprops/action-gh-release@v2 with download table ✓
8. Publish: dry-run only (actual crates.io publishes commented out) ✓

FINDINGS:
- Workflow has NEVER been triggered (gh run list --workflow=release.yml returns empty)
- No tags or releases exist in the repo
- Only 4 connectors built (anthropic, discord, openai, telegram) — ~146 others omitted
- fcp-host and fcp-cron binaries not included in release (only fwc CLI)
- Publish step is dry-run only — crates.io tokens not configured
- ubuntu-24.04-arm runner may not be available in all GitHub tiers

CANNOT VERIFY WITHOUT TRIGGER:
- Whether the workflow actually succeeds on GitHub Actions (nightly toolchain + workspace deps)
- Whether cosign signing works with OIDC in this repo's configuration
- Whether the 45-minute timeout is sufficient for cross-compilation

RECOMMENDATION: Cannot close this bead without actually triggering the workflow. This requires creating a tag or using workflow_dispatch, which is a user action (pushing to the remote). The workflow definition appears structurally correct but is untested.","created_at":"2026-04-19T02:47:21Z"},{"id":2749,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh.4","author":"jemanuel","text":"SunnyMoose deep verification of release.yml (2026-04-19):

CRITICAL BUG FOUND AND FIXED (7f24fd41):
All 5 release targets (fwc, fcp-anthropic, fcp-discord, fcp-openai, fcp-telegram) depend on two external repos via local path:
- asupersync at ../../../asupersync (7 crates: fcp-async-core, fcp-host, fcp-telemetry, fcp-tailscale, fcp-graphql, discord, slack)
- toon_rust at ../../../toon_rust (fwc only, via 'tru' package)
These paths would not resolve on GitHub Actions CI. Fixed by adding shallow git clones in all 3 build jobs.

LOCAL VERIFICATION (macOS ARM64):
1. Release build fwc: OK (7.3 MB, 2m32s)
2. Release build fcp-anthropic: OK (4.0 MB)
3. Release build fcp-discord: OK (5.4 MB)
4. Release build fcp-openai: OK (4.1 MB)
5. Release build fcp-telegram: OK (4.9 MB)
6. tar.xz packaging: OK (5 archives, 1.5-3.1 MB each)
7. SHA256 checksums: OK (verified round-trip)
8. Combined SHA256SUMS.txt: OK

REMAINING ITEMS (cannot verify without triggering workflow):
- Cross-platform builds (Linux x64/ARM64, macOS x64, Windows x64)
- Cosign OIDC keyless signing (requires GitHub Actions OIDC provider)
- ubuntu-24.04-arm runner availability (GA since Jan 2026)
- 45-minute timeout sufficiency for cold-cache builds
- softprops/action-gh-release@v2 release creation

RECOMMENDATION: Workflow is now structurally ready for a test trigger via workflow_dispatch. The user should run: gh workflow run release.yml -f version=0.0.1-test","created_at":"2026-04-19T03:18:03Z"},{"id":2759,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh.4","author":"jemanuel","text":"Handing back on 2026-04-19: latest GitHub Actions release run is still the stale queued run 24631366023 on commit 0daecf680f70ce95ea86f89ccf4bb162773108e3 (queued		Release	Release	main	workflow_dispatch	24631366023	46m45s	2026-04-19T14:27:42Z). It is not green, so the bead cannot be closed. Local fixes now exist for both blockers discovered in that run: Linux dbus build deps in .github/workflows/release.yml (commit 79a58bda) and Windows Rust 2024 HANDLE/unsafe extern fixes in crates/fcp-sandbox/src/windows.rs (commit 2d2fd519). The current blocker is that GitHub has not executed a new post-fix release run yet; rerun release.yml after those commits are on the remote branch, then verify all platform legs, signing, checksums, and release asset publication before reclosing lopoh.4.","created_at":"2026-04-19T15:14:27Z"},{"id":2760,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh.4","author":"jemanuel","text":"Investigating the still-stale release dispatch. The only live run remains 24631366023 on 0daecf6; Linux and Windows failures in that run are the pre-fix failures, and both x86_64-apple-darwin jobs are still queued more than an hour later on macos-13. I am patching .github/workflows/release.yml to route the x86_64-apple-darwin target through macos-14 instead so the release proof no longer depends on a separate Intel macOS runner becoming available.","created_at":"2026-04-19T15:29:39Z"},{"id":2763,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh.4","author":"jemanuel","text":"2026-04-19 follow-up: latest `gh run list --workflow=release --limit 3` still shows only stale queued run 24631366023, so lopoh.4 is not closable yet. The requested fallback beads are unavailable in the current graph: d5yeb is already closed and z1nkz.4 does not exist. Pulling the current ready set now for the next actionable task while release proof remains blocked on a fresh post-fix dispatch.","created_at":"2026-04-19T15:52:06Z"},{"id":2764,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh.4","author":"jemanuel","text":"Current state after recheck: gh run list --workflow=release --limit 3 still shows only queued run 24631366023 on head 0daecf680f70ce95ea86f89ccf4bb162773108e3. None of the known release-fix commits are on any remote branch yet: 79a58bda (Linux dbus deps), 2d2fd519 (Windows sandbox compile), 7eeff127 (route x86_64-apple-darwin target through macos-14). lopoh.4 therefore remains blocked on the absence of any fresh post-fix release dispatch, not on a newly observed runtime failure.","created_at":"2026-04-19T15:52:33Z"},{"id":2766,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh.4","author":"jemanuel","text":"Additional CI blocker fixed on 2026-04-19: .github/workflows/release.yml previously used top-level concurrency  with . Because the only existing dispatch 24631366023 is stuck queued on old head 0daecf6, that policy can pin the entire release lane and prevent a fresh post-fix retry on main from replacing it. I changed the workflow to  and  so a new manual dispatch on main cancels the stale queued run instead of waiting behind it forever.","created_at":"2026-04-19T15:58:20Z"},{"id":2768,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh.4","author":"jemanuel","text":"2026-04-19 hand-back: gh run list --workflow=release --limit 3 still shows only stale queued run 24631366023 on old head 0daecf680f70ce95ea86f89ccf4bb162773108e3. There is no fresh post-fix release run to inspect, so there is no new failure beyond the stale pre-fix Linux dbus / Windows sandbox / Intel-mac queue issues already addressed locally. Specific blocker: none of the CI-fix commits have been exercised on remote because no new release dispatch has started on a branch containing 79a58bda, 2d2fd519, 7eeff127, and 7d2fbe7d. This bead should be retried only after a fresh `release.yml` run is launched from a remote ref containing those commits.","created_at":"2026-04-19T16:08:23Z"},{"id":2780,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh.4","author":"jemanuel","text":"Swarm controller (2026-04-19): flagged as sole remaining open bead. Deferring to user. Swarm is now in pure code-review mode on the existing codebase.","created_at":"2026-04-19T20:29:43Z"}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh.5","title":"[Registry] Test supply chain rejection: tampered binary, expired signature, wrong platform all caught","description":"Negative tests for supply chain verification: 1) Flip a byte in signed binary -> install MUST fail with signature verification error, 2) Sign with expired timestamp -> install MUST fail with expired attestation error, 3) Present wrong platform binary (linux on macOS) -> install MUST fail with platform mismatch, 4) Present binary with capabilities not in zone ceiling -> install MUST fail with capability subset violation, 5) All failures should produce structured errors (not panics or generic messages). These prove the README's supply chain verification pipeline actually CATCHES attacks, not just that it verifies valid packages.","notes":"Progress 2026-04-07: strengthened negative coverage to assert structured errors for tampered binary, wrong platform, capability ceiling violation, SLSA insufficiency, missing attestation evidence, and untrusted builder; added explicit expired-attestation rejection path in fcp-registry via AttestationEvidence.expires_at + RegistryError::AttestationExpired, plus integration and unit tests for expiry; MockTufVerifier now also rejects expired TUF metadata. Fresh rch cargo check/test runs are in flight against the updated code.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-06T21:43:11.666886Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-07T06:12:07.078783Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T06:12:07.078553Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["registry","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh.5","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-06T21:43:11.666886Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh.5","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-lopoh.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-06T21:43:12.350098Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

-{"id":"flywheel_connectors-lse4w","title":"README overstates Zone Isolation as PROVEN while host zone binding stays permissive by default","description":"Evidence:
- README.md:51 labels Zone Isolation as PROVEN and describes cryptographic isolation + enforcement.
- crates/fcp-host/src/bin/fcp-host.rs:539-545 documents that an empty allowed_zones set means no binding was configured and the host stays on a back-compat permissive path.
- crates/fcp-host/src/bin/fcp-host.rs:1446-1448 says live enforcement is opt-in and that empty allowed_zones preserves pre-binding behavior for existing inventories.

Impact:
- The top-level README claim reads stronger than the current host-backed operator path actually enforces. Zone isolation is proven in core primitives, but live host routing still has a configuration-dependent permissive gap.
- This is a truthfulness / architectural narrative mismatch between README and the current implementation story described in AGENTS.md and fcp-host itself.

Fix shape:
- Narrow the README claim to core/type-level proof only, or explicitly qualify that host-side connector zone binding is enforced only when allowed_zones is configured.
- Keep the status language aligned with the current transitional host-first path.","status":"in_progress","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:32:37.496145Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:34:56.446159Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"flywheel_connectors-lse4w","title":"README overstates Zone Isolation as PROVEN while host zone binding stays permissive by default","description":"Evidence:
- README.md:51 labels Zone Isolation as PROVEN and describes cryptographic isolation + enforcement.
- crates/fcp-host/src/bin/fcp-host.rs:539-545 documents that an empty allowed_zones set means no binding was configured and the host stays on a back-compat permissive path.
- crates/fcp-host/src/bin/fcp-host.rs:1446-1448 says live enforcement is opt-in and that empty allowed_zones preserves pre-binding behavior for existing inventories.

Impact:
- The top-level README claim reads stronger than the current host-backed operator path actually enforces. Zone isolation is proven in core primitives, but live host routing still has a configuration-dependent permissive gap.
- This is a truthfulness / architectural narrative mismatch between README and the current implementation story described in AGENTS.md and fcp-host itself.

Fix shape:
- Narrow the README claim to core/type-level proof only, or explicitly qualify that host-side connector zone binding is enforced only when allowed_zones is configured.
- Keep the status language aligned with the current transitional host-first path.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:32:37.496145Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:36:04.075320Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:36:04.074889Z","close_reason":"README zone isolation status now reflects host-side allowed_zones opt-in enforcement semantics","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-lszk","title":"[FCP2] Connector Library: V2-Compliant Connectors","description":"# [FCP2] Connector Library: V2-Compliant Connectors

## Goal
Build a library of production connectors that **fully comply** with the FCP V2 platform:
- single-zone binding
- default deny via capabilities
- revocation freshness enforcement
- correct receipts and audit events
- sandbox + network constraints
- no secrets on disk; no secret logging

This epic intentionally assumes the **core platform** exists first.

## Entry criteria (what must exist before connectors are real)
Connectors should not begin until these are available:
- `[FCP2] Connector SDK (Rust)`
- `[FCP2] Connector Manifest (TOML)`
- `[FCP2] Policy Engine` (DecisionReceipts + reason codes)
- `[FCP2] Sandbox + Egress Proxy`
- `[FCP2] MeshNode` (enforcement boundary)
- `[FCP2] Registry + Supply Chain` (install/verify)
- `[FCP2] Conformance` (golden vectors + fuzz + compliance runner)

## Definition of Done
- Each connector has:
  - a manifest with explicit capabilities + sandbox profile
  - a conformance test suite (unit + integration with mocks)
  - E2E compliance run output
  - performance checks where applicable
  - no real external API calls in unit tests

## Notes
- Backwards compatibility is not a goal. Implement the V2 model directly.
- Mechanical compliance checking is part of the conformance suite.

## Success Criteria
- Every connector epic follows `flywheel_connectors-lszk.5` (base beads `.1`..`.4`, state model, NetworkConstraints, etc.).
- Every connector has mock-only unit/integration coverage per `flywheel_connectors-h32`.
- Tier-1 connectors (Telegram, Discord, OpenAI, Anthropic) each have:
  - a complete manifest/capability map
  - provisioning automation (where applicable)
  - passing E2E compliance runs (`flywheel_connectors-e3i9` + `flywheel_connectors-1n78.21.5`).
- No connector requires hard-coded pricing tables; usage metrics are emitted and policy can compute costs externally.

cks","created_at":"2026-03-02T18:31:41Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-lszk","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.31","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-02T18:31:41Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-lszk","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.33","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-02T18:31:41Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1666,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-lszk","author":"jemanuel","text":"MistyPond:  {"id":"flywheel_connectors-lszk.1","title":"fcp.telegram - Telegram Connector (V2)","description":"# fcp.telegram - Telegram Connector (FCP2)

## Goal
Provide a **fully FCP2-compliant** Telegram Bot integration for secure automation in high-risk chat surfaces.

This connector must be:
- **Single-zone bound** for its lifetime (one instance ↔ one `ZoneId`).
- **Default-deny**: every external action requires explicit CapabilityTokens.
- **Mechanically sandboxed**: NetworkConstraints + OS/WASI sandbox enforced by the host.
- **Secrets-safe**: bot tokens never touch disk; never appear in logs.

## Manifest archetypes (closed set)
- `bidirectional` (send + receive)
- `streaming` (message/update events)
- `operational` (request/response actions)

> Note: polling/webhooks are *interaction patterns*, not manifest archetype values.

## Threat model (why this connector is special)
Telegram content is an adversarial prompt surface.

Hard requirements:
- All inbound Telegram content is tainted as **external input** by default.
- The connector must emit provenance/taint metadata so policy can enforce:
  - deny-by-default cross-zone data movement
  - declassification only with explicit ApprovalToken scope
  - sanitizer receipts for any “cleaning” step

## Zone model (mechanical)

<!-- fcp2-single-zone-note -->
**FCP2 single-zone binding note:** A connector instance binds to exactly one `ZoneId` for its entire lifetime.
The *"zones"* described in this section are a **recommended deployment pattern** (run separate connector instances in separate zones with different capability grants/policies) and/or conceptual risk tiers.
The connector MUST NOT multiplex multiple zones internally.

Each connector instance binds to exactly one zone. Recommended zone naming conventions (policy-owned, not connector-owned):
- `z:community:telegram:<group_or_guild>` (semi-trusted communities)
- `z:public:telegram:<channel>` (public channels)
- `z:owner:telegram:<dm>` (explicitly opted-in DMs)

## External surface
### NetworkConstraints (MUST)
- Allow egress only to Telegram Bot API endpoints:
  - `api.telegram.org:443`
- Deny IP literals by default; require hostname canonicalization.
- No raw sockets in strict/moderate sandboxes; all egress goes through the Network Guard.

### Auth / credential injection
- Bot token provided via FCP2 `CredentialObject`/`CredentialId` injection (egress proxy preferred).
- Never persist token (or derived bearer headers) to disk.

## Capability model (illustrative, final IDs defined in manifest bead)
**NORMATIVE constraint:** capability IDs must not encode hosts/ports.

Capability families:
- `telegram.messages.read` (receive/poll updates; creates EventEnvelopes)
- `telegram.messages.send` (side-effecting send)
- `telegram.media.read` (download attachments)
- `telegram.media.send` (upload media)
- `telegram.chats.read` (list/get chat metadata)
- `telegram.members.read` (chat member info; treat as PII)
- `telegram.webhooks.manage` (Dangerous; requires explicit policy + ApprovalToken `Execution` scope)

## Operations & event topics (initial slice)
Keep the first slice tight and correct; expand after conformance is stable.

### Operations
- `telegram.send_message` (side-effect; requires `telegram.messages.send`)
- `telegram.send_media` (side-effect; requires `telegram.media.send`)
- `telegram.get_chat` / `telegram.list_chats` (read-only; requires `telegram.chats.read`)
- `telegram.get_updates` (streaming source; requires `telegram.messages.read`)
- `telegram.download_file` (read-only; requires `telegram.media.read`)

### Event topics
- `telegram.message.new`
- `telegram.message.edited`
- `telegram.chat.member_joined`
- `telegram.chat.member_left`

## State model
Telegram polling is inherently stateful.

- Use **singleton-writer** state with a lease (to avoid two instances consuming the same update stream).
- Persist cursor/offset in a state object (e.g., last `update_id` + replay/ack cursor).
- Side-effect idempotency:
  - Prefer **fail-closed** on uncertain outcomes (timeout after request sent) to avoid duplicate sends.
  - When the remote API provides a definitive message id response, bind it to an OperationReceipt.

## Rate limiting & timeouts
- Enforce Telegram Bot API limits mechanically (connector + platform). No “best effort”.
- All network calls must be bounded by timeouts; no unbounded long-polls outside allowed sandbox policy.

## Observability & audit
- Structured logs with redaction (no token, no raw message bodies by default).
- Emit AuditEvents for:
  - credential injection usage
  - side-effecting sends
  - webhook configuration changes
  - repeated denials / policy violations

## Test plan (must be mechanical)
- **Unit tests**: pure logic (parsers, schema validation, state machine) + wiremock HTTP client.
- **Integration tests**: deterministic fake Bot API server producing scripted updates.
- **E2E compliance**: run through the Mechanical Connector Compliance Runner:
  - `--manifest` correctness (archetypes, NetworkConstraints, capability map)
  - default deny (no token → denial + DecisionReceipt)
  - sandbox/network enforcement (deny any non-telegram host)
  - taint/provenance labeling on inbound events
  - structured JSON logs with correlation IDs

## Acceptance Criteria
- Passes connector compliance runner (static + dynamic).
- Passes integration tests without real Telegram calls.
- Meets cold-start and steady-state budgets (target: <50ms cold start; <10MB idle).
","notes":"2026-03-03 WhiteBay: Implemented Telegram polling singleton-writer lease fencing + durable cursor persistence in connectors/telegram, added/updated tests, validated via rch exec check/clippy/test (pass).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"WhiteBay","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T06:24:04.685605437Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-03-03T02:28:01.503308Z","closed_at":"2026-03-03T02:28:01.503270Z","close_reason":"Completed Telegram connector V2 scope.  {"id":"flywheel_connectors-lszk.1.1","title":"fcp.telegram: Manifest + Capability Map (FCP2)","description":"# fcp.telegram: Manifest + Capability Map (FCP2)

## Goal
Define the mechanical contract for `fcp.telegram` so the platform can sandbox, authorize, and test it without ambiguity.

This bead produces the Telegram connector's manifest inputs:
- operation list + schema IDs
- capability IDs + per-operation required caps
- NetworkConstraints for each operation
- state model declaration (`singleton_writer`)
- event topics + replay/ack model

## Canonical manifest example (GOOD)
```toml
[manifest]
format = "fcp-connector-manifest"
version = "2"
interface_hash = "sha256:__PLACEHOLDER__"

[connector]
connector_id = "telegram:bidirectional:v1"
name = "fcp.telegram"
version = "1.0.0"
archetypes = ["bidirectional", "streaming", "operational"]
state_model = "singleton_writer"

[zones]
home = "z:community"
allowed_sources = ["z:community", "z:work"]
forbidden = ["z:owner"]

[capabilities]
required = [
  "ipc.gateway",
  "network.dns",
  "network.outbound",
  "network.tls.sni",
  "network.tls.spki_pin",
  "storage.persistent:encrypted",
]
optional = ["media.download", "media.upload"]
forbidden = ["system.exec", "network.inbound"]

[provides.operations."telegram.send_message"]
description = "Send a message to a Telegram chat"
capability = "telegram.messages.send"
risk_level = "medium"
safety_tier = "risky"
requires_approval = "policy"
rate_limit = "60/min"
idempotency = "best_effort"
input_schema = { type = "object", required = ["chat_id", "text"], properties = { chat_id = { type = "string" }, text = { type = "string" }, parse_mode = { type = "string" }, reply_to_message_id = { type = "integer" } } }
output_schema = { type = "object", required = ["message_id", "chat_id"], properties = { message_id = { type = "integer" }, chat_id = { type = "string" } } }
network_constraints = { host_allow = ["api.telegram.org"], port_allow = [443], require_sni = true, deny_ip_literals = true, max_redirects = 0, connect_timeout_ms = 5000, total_timeout_ms = 60000, max_response_bytes = 1048576 }

[provides.operations."telegram.send_message".ai_hints]
when_to_use = "Use to post updates to approved chats."
common_mistakes = ["Sending secrets", "Responding to tainted inputs"]

[provides.operations."telegram.get_file"]
description = "Fetch Telegram file metadata and download URL"
capability = "telegram.media.read"
risk_level = "low"
safety_tier = "safe"
requires_approval = "none"
rate_limit = "120/min"
idempotency = "idempotent"
input_schema = { type = "object", required = ["file_id"], properties = { file_id = { type = "string" } } }
output_schema = { type = "object", required = ["file_id"], properties = { file_id = { type = "string" }, file_unique_id = { type = "string" }, file_size = { type = "integer" }, file_path = { type = "string" }, download_url = { type = "string" } } }
network_constraints = { host_allow = ["api.telegram.org"], port_allow = [443], require_sni = true, deny_ip_literals = true, max_redirects = 0, connect_timeout_ms = 5000, total_timeout_ms = 60000, max_response_bytes = 1048576 }

[provides.operations."telegram.answer_callback_query"]
description = "Acknowledge a callback query (button press)"
capability = "telegram.messages.send"
risk_level = "low"
safety_tier = "safe"
requires_approval = "none"
rate_limit = "120/min"
idempotency = "idempotent"
input_schema = { type = "object", required = ["callback_query_id"], properties = { callback_query_id = { type = "string" }, text = { type = "string" } } }
output_schema = { type = "object", required = ["success"], properties = { success = { type = "boolean" } } }
network_constraints = { host_allow = ["api.telegram.org"], port_allow = [443], require_sni = true, deny_ip_literals = true, max_redirects = 0, connect_timeout_ms = 5000, total_timeout_ms = 60000, max_response_bytes = 1048576 }

[event_caps]
streaming = true
replay = false
min_buffer_events = 1000

[sandbox]
profile = "strict"            # "strict", "strict_plus", "moderate", or "permissive"
memory_mb = 256
cpu_percent = 50
wall_clock_timeout_ms = 30000
fs_readonly_paths = ["/usr", "/lib"]
fs_writable_paths = ["$CONNECTOR_STATE"]
deny_exec = true
deny_ptrace = true

[signatures]
publisher_signatures = [
  { kid = "pubkey1", sig = "base64:..." },
  { kid = "pubkey2", sig = "base64:..." },
]
publisher_threshold = "2-of-3"
registry_signature = { kid = "registry1", sig = "base64:..." }
transparency_log_entry = "objectid:..."
```

## Rejected examples (BAD)
```toml
# BAD: network capability encodes host:port (must use network_constraints)
[capabilities]
required = ["network.outbound:api.telegram.org:443"]
```

```toml
# BAD: streaming enabled but min_buffer_events = 0
[event_caps]
streaming = true
replay = false
min_buffer_events = 0
```

```toml
# BAD: IP literal allowed and redirect policy too loose
[provides.operations."telegram.send_message"]
network_constraints = { host_allow = ["1.2.3.4"], port_allow = [443], deny_ip_literals = false, max_redirects = 10 }
```

## Event topics (contract)
- `telegram.message.new`
- `telegram.message.edited`
- `telegram.channel_post.new`
- `telegram.channel_post.edited`
- `telegram.callback_query`

## Event payload schema (JSON shape)
- `telegram.message.*` / `telegram.channel_post.*` payload:
  - `message_id` (integer), `chat` (object), `from` (object), `date` (integer epoch seconds)
  - `text` (string, optional), `caption` (string, optional)
  - `has_photo` / `has_document` / `has_audio` / `has_video` / `has_voice` (boolean)
  - `reply_to_message_id` (integer, optional), `message_thread_id` (integer, optional)
- `telegram.callback_query` payload:
  - `id` (string), `from` (object), `data` (string, optional), `chat_instance` (string)

## Cursor semantics
`update_id` is the monotonic cursor/seq source; replay is currently false and acks are not required.

## Capability families (contract)
- `telegram.messages.read` (Safe) - inbound updates / polling
- `telegram.messages.send` (Risky) - send messages, answer callback queries
- `telegram.media.read` (Safe) - fetch file metadata / download URL
- `telegram.media.send` (Risky) - upload/send media
- `telegram.chats.read` (Safe) - chat metadata
- `telegram.members.read` (Safe) - member lists
- `telegram.webhooks.manage` (Dangerous) - webhook configuration

## Acceptance Criteria
- Manifest spec is complete enough to implement `--manifest` without external docs.
- Mechanical Connector Compliance Runner has everything it needs to validate Telegram statically.
